nose: Upon the first nosing, intense aromas of ashy, cold campfire smoke emerge, accompanied by earthy notes. A subtle sweetness of honey and vanilla harmonizes with a pronounced sea breeze reminiscent of salty seawater. This combination imparts deep complexity and authenticity to the whisky.
palate: On the palate, the whisky is oily and full-bodied. Fresh citrus notes, especially lemon zest, meet chalky hints and a touch of medicinal iodine, reminiscent of bandages. The voluminous smoke is complemented by nuances of heather and a slight nuttiness, adding depth and complexity to the whisky.
finish: The finish is medium-length, characterized by a salty, seawater-like note. A light fruit sweetness emerges, while the lingering campfire smoke provides a warming conclusion. The complexity of the finish both surprises and delights.
conclusion: A whisky that immediately captivates with its smoky campfire aroma and salty sea breeze – here you can taste the wild soul of Islay. The Dun Naomhaig Bay Batch 1 is not a light dram, but a characterful one that’s great fun and tells new stories with every nosing.

product_name: Tormore 12 Years (2011) Spiced vanilla chai cake Cask 105.45 - The Scotch Malt Whisky Society
nose: A lot is happening here. A mixture of juicy apple and ripe pear meets an unexpected, almost maritime freshness. The whole is accompanied by a perfumed note reminiscent of time-honoured wood polish or even a noble sandalwood scent. This is joined by caramel, a hint of chalk dust and a subtle stony sweetness. If you take your time, you will discover ever new facets - a multi-layered bouquet that balances between sweet and mineral.
palate: Expecting a certain heaviness on the nose, the whisky is surprisingly light-footed and sweet. An intense sugariness spreads across the tongue, almost as if someone had placed a spoonful of caramelised malt sugar on the taste buds. This is accompanied by light-coloured fruit, a subtle maltiness and an unusual spiciness - a little like a rustic rye whisky, but with more elegance. Interestingly, the sweetness is reminiscent of those dark, brown vodkas that fascinate with their syrupy texture.
finish: This is where the Tormore shows its edgier side. The finish is rather short, but brings with it a salty breeze - a final nod to the maritime notes from the nosing. Spicy accents, a hint of oak and a gentle peppery tingle finish pleasantly.
conclusion: This Tormore 2011 is a playful yet sophisticated dram that delights with its sweet spice and maritime freshness – not a whisky for cozy beginner evenings, but for connoisseurs eager to explore something new. The combination of vanilla, chai, and vibrant fruitiness creates a character that lifts the mood and leaves you curious for more.

nose: The flavour of this whisky initially offers a pleasant sweetness with hints of ripe fruit and vanilla. This light, tantalising note is complemented by creamy milk chocolate, which promises a good start. However, less typical elements then come into play: a slightly grassy hay note and a hint of yoghurt add interesting facets, but are not entirely harmonious. Another point that polarises: a subtle spiciness reminiscent of paprika. Whether this is perceived as positive or irritating is probably in the eye (or rather the nose) of the beholder.
palate: The first sip immediately reveals the high alcohol content. The whisky is strong, almost sharp, and the flavours initially struggle to develop. Vanilla and a certain fruitiness are noticeable, but they do not really manage to balance out the intensity of the alcohol. A dry impression, accompanied by a hint of chocolate, provides a certain depth. Nevertheless, the overall picture remains rather unbalanced - the individual components seem to stand next to each other instead of harmonising with each other.
finish: The finish of the Auchroisk is characterised by distinct oak, which is strong and slightly bitter. There is a delicate fruity note reminiscent of redcurrants, but it remains in the background and is not very pronounced. Milk chocolate is also present here, albeit rather discreetly.
conclusion: With the Auchroisk 2010/2023, you experience a rough but honest malt that primarily delivers drinking pleasure through its sweetness and chocolate notes – even though the high alcohol can sometimes be a bit much. Not a masterpiece, but a whisky that shows character and piques curiosity to perhaps discover even more with the next dram.

nose: The initial impression is characterized by a mild and rounded aroma. Notes of apricot, vanilla, and apple blend harmoniously, complemented by a hint of grassiness and walnut, rounding out the bouquet.
palate: On the palate, the whisky opens with the freshness of young strawberries, followed by gentle red fruits and aged raisins. A slightly winey character intertwines with caramel and star anise notes, adding complexity.
finish: The finish is lightly woody with intense ginger spiciness and a lingering raspberry brandy essence.
conclusion: The HeiligenBergFeld 2020/2023 brings a lot of drinking pleasure to the glass with its lively fruitiness and spicy ginger heat, even though its still-young maturation time denies it a bit more depth. For fans of German new-school whiskies, it’s a charming introduction with potential that leaves you wanting more.

nose: Right from the start, this whisky delivers a whirlwind of aromas. Plums, figs, and dates create an intense sweetness, while morello cherries add a darker fruit note. There are also hints of leather, tobacco, and something reminiscent of a long-heated grain pillow – warm, spicy, and slightly nutty.
palate: The first sip is a full-on sherry explosion. Sweet, fruity flavors dominate the tip of the tongue before transitioning into old leather sofas, Cognac-like notes, and tobacco leaves. The texture is creamy, almost oily, yet it quickly dries out. Aged oak brings a strong spiciness, while slightly burnt notes add a smoky-bitter accent. Somewhere in between, rust and machine oil play a subordinate role.
finish: Long and intense. The oak spice builds up further, but the sweetness remains – this time more like heather honey and caramel. The whisky fades out with dry, spicy oak, leaving a lingering warmth.
conclusion: This Craigellachie 2013/2024 is like a wild rollercoaster ride for the senses – untamed, full-bodied, and packed with plenty of rough edges. If you’re into powerful sherry bombs, you’ll have a blast with every nose and every sip. Elegance is nowhere to be found here, but that’s exactly what gives it its charm!

product_name: Goa Indian Whisky Ratafia Champenois Cask T-073
nose: You'll notice it from the very first sniff: This is not about standard notes! A slightly musty red fruit note starts things off - a bit like dried cherries stored in an old wooden box. But then exciting contrasts come into play: fresh leather mixes with brown sugar, joined by a clear orange note. A fragrance that is both exotic and familiar at the same time.
palate: Velvety and flattering! This whisky is a real charmer - sweet but not sticky, fruity but not overloaded. Vanilla and ripe fruit dance on the tongue, while a hint of leather provides an exciting depth. The sweetness is almost reminiscent of sugar syrup, but thanks to the fine spice, everything remains beautifully balanced.
finish: The finish of the Goa Indian Whisky has a gentle, almost creamy flavour with a hint of strawberries. Woody nuances ensure a successful finish - soft, but with enough character.
conclusion: This whisky is a true delight for anyone who enjoys sweet and velvety flavors without slipping into dullness. The unusual combination of tropical maturation and French Ratafia cask aging gives it a charming depth that’s a lot of fun – perfect for relaxed evenings with friends.

nose: Raise your glass and you are immediately transported into a world of cold smoke and gentle sea spray. There is a slight hint of iodine, just as you would expect from a Lagavulin. But there is more: a gentle vanilla sweetness provides balance, accompanied by subtle citrus accents and an almost floral freshness. A hint of menthol-like ice candy flashes up and brings a cool, slightly minty note into play. The flavour is unmistakably Islay, but with a little Offerman twist.
palate: The smoke remains present on the tongue, but it does not dominate the entire experience. Instead, the whisky opens with a pleasant fruitiness - pear and apple provide a subtle sweetness that elegantly complements the smoky foundation. The texture is soft, almost creamy, with a slightly ashy undertone reminiscent of cooled charcoal. Lagavulin shows a somewhat more accessible side here, without losing its Islay character.
finish: The finish is long, voluminous and expressive. Oaky notes prevail, accompanied by a gentle peppery flavour - white pepper briefly tickles the palate before a hint of icing sugar joins in and rounds off the finish with a mild sweetness. It is a classic Islay finish, but without the brutal force that some younger bottlings bring.
conclusion: This Lagavulin 2024 Offerman is like a familiar friend around the campfire – smoky, warm, and pleasantly fruity, with the joy of drinking clearly taking center stage. It doesn’t bring any big surprises, but that’s exactly what makes it the perfect companion for cozy evenings when you just want to savor the moment.

product_name: St. Kilian Grave Digger Erdfassreifung Exhumation – Chapter II – Peated
nose: Surprisingly little peat opens the nose, instead earthy tones, fresh wood, and a certain fruitiness dominate. An intense, almost tangible freshness permeates the aroma, sparking curiosity for the first sip.
palate: The whisky presents itself oily and spicy on the palate, accompanied by notes of beetroot, vanilla, and caramel. A subtle smoky note resonates, while earthy, root-like elements round off the impression. The dryness in the finish adds extra structure and depth.
finish: The finish is long and voluminous. Earthy, slightly bitter notes of root vegetables come to the forefront, while smoky and steamy hints linger. The balance between sweetness, spiciness, and bitterness is impressive.
conclusion: Those who embrace St. Kilian’s Exhumation – Chapter II embark on a wild, earthy journey with surprisingly little smoke, a dram that will especially delight lovers of unusual flavors. Its complexity and intense mouthfeel make it a true highlight for anyone who sees whisky as an adventure. Not exactly an entry-level dram, but definitely a whisky with a strong character in the glass.

nose: The first sniff takes you on a journey: mildly smoky notes of BBQ and ham blend with red fruits, including blackcurrants, adding a fresh and fruity touch. Hints of vanilla and caramel provide a pleasant sweetness, harmoniously rounding off the bouquet.
palate: On the palate, the 14-years Port Charlotte reveals its full-bodied complexity. The subtle smoke remains present, accompanied by light earthy tones and a hint of maritime breeze, reminiscent of salty waves. The BBQ sauce and ham notes from the nose return, adding memorable depth to the flavor.
finish: The finish is long and layered. Spicy oak notes dominate at first, gradually giving way to a light fruity sweetness, providing a well-rounded conclusion.
conclusion: This Port Charlotte is like a cheerful evening around the campfire: spicy, smoky, and with a pleasant sweetness that never becomes overpowering. For anyone who enjoys character and a touch of saltiness, this is a genuine taste experience. Simply a whisky that lifts your spirits and leaves you wanting more!

nose: The first impression is pleasant and layered. Malty notes form the foundation, while dark chocolate and fruity hints of apple and pear add variety. Vanilla subtly lingers in the background, providing a creamy touch. The nose is not overly intense but present and inviting.
palate: The initial sip is distinctly malty before a refreshing citrus note takes over. Cinnamon brings a certain spiciness that blends with toffee and a mix of light and dark fruits. The “wild fruit mix” is particularly intriguing, difficult to pinpoint but adding a dynamic character to the whisky. A spicy oak note provides structure, and the texture has a slight tingle – a pleasant interplay between sweetness and spice.
finish: The finish is marked by solid oak notes, yet remains pleasantly rounded due to soft tannins. A light ginger kick adds a fresh spiciness, while the aftertaste fades slowly with a mix of wood and warm spices.
conclusion: Here you find a whisky that’s simply fun with its hint of cinnamon and wild fruit mix – not a top-tier delight, but a likeable companion for cozy evenings. If you appreciate character and drinkability, this one’s definitely worth reaching for.

nose: On the nose, the whisky is accessible and well-balanced. Caramel and vanilla provide a sweet foundation, while a bright fruit basket of apple, pear, and peach adds freshness. A subtle grain note of oats and malt brings depth, complemented by a touch of candied orange peel. A faint wisp of smoke lingers in the background, giving the blend a hint of extra character.
palate: On the palate, the Johnnie Walker 18 Years initially feels somewhat thin but remains smooth and silky. The flavors are well-balanced: orange marmalade brings fruity sweetness, accompanied by dark fruits and peach. Surprisingly, the malty note is quite present for a blend. The smoke stays subtle, with an earthy, slightly ashy touch that adds a bit of complexity.
finish: The finish is medium in length, marked by pleasant bitter orange notes. A touch of wood emerges without becoming overpowering. The smoke provides a hint of volume but remains in the background.
conclusion: A smooth, pleasant blend that, with its gentle sweetness and subtle smoke, feels almost like a good friend – uncomplicated and likable, but without much depth. Anyone looking for a relaxed whisky for cozy moments will surely be happy here. For true characterful personalities, however, there are more exciting options.

nose: The first impression? Vanilla meets mint - as if someone had thrown a bourbon into a herb garden. Then apple and peach come into play, accompanied by a fine acidity that lends freshness to the whole. Red grapes and honey round off the flavour, while a slightly alcoholic note lurks in the background (a little cheeky, but charming). Anyone who likes sweet, fruity yet invigorating whiskies will immediately find their nose stuck in the glass.
palate: The first sip? Stone fruit at full volume! Peach, apricot and a little nectarine - as if someone had distilled fruit salad in liquid form. This is joined by a load of vanilla, freshly picked herbs and a hint of grapefruit, which provides a tangy freshness. Malty? And how! Anyone familiar with Nc'Nean's Organic Flagship will immediately feel at home here - just in an even rounder, fuller-flavoured version.
finish: Where other whiskies end with strong oak, the Huntress prefers to remain fresh, fruity and berry-flavoured. The tannins remain in the background while the fruitiness gently fades away. Anyone who has ever tasted Nc'Nean's standard bottling will notice the difference - the Orchard Cobbler is softer and smoother without losing its characteristic freshness.
conclusion: A lively delight that, with its fruity lightness, really lifts the spirits – perfect for anyone not looking for a heavy hitter, but a whisky with charm and character. Nc’nean shows here just how much fun experimentation can be without ever becoming dull.

product_name: The Balvenie 14 Years (2009) A Collection of Curious Casks
nose: The first sniff reveals a surprisingly dirty, maritime character, reminiscent of salty sea air and damp stones. However, clear honey and vanilla tones quickly join in, giving the whisky a sweet base. Light citrus peel and a hint of star anise round off the bouquet and give it an exotic flavour.
palate: On the tongue, theSingle Malt unfolds as a light yet complex whisky. The sweetness of vanilla dominates at first, followed by a citrusy freshness reminiscent of lime and grapefruit. A chalky minerality pervades the flavour and gives it structure. Notes of light-coloured fallen fruit meadow and fresh grass resonate in the background, lending the whisky a summery lightness.
finish: The finish is long and pleasant, with a distinct oaky spiciness accompanied by earthy and loamy nuances. The maritime, almost dirty character returns and leaves a lasting impression.
conclusion: With its fruity lightness and maritime notes, the Balvenie 2009 is a true delight for relaxed evenings – a whisky that sparks curiosity and is simply fun to enjoy. It surprises with character and a pleasant sweetness that invites you back for more without overwhelming the palate.

nose: From the very first nosing, the aroma of cold ashes rises to the nose, as if the campfire had just stopped smouldering. There is also a subtle hint of iodine, reminiscent of a fresh sea breeze. The fruity accents of green apple and lemon balm, which blend gently into the smoke, are surprising. A subtle vanilla note rounds off the flavour and gives it a slight, subliminal sweetness.
palate: The whisky is oily and warming on the tongue. The ash dominance from the nose continues, paired with a dry, chalky note. This is joined by a hint of unripe peach, which adds a subtle freshness, while a fine saltiness is reminiscent of sea spray. The flavours are powerful, direct and uncompromising - typically Islay.
finish: Kilchoman remains true to itself in the finish: voluminous smoke dominates, accompanied by a light oak note that gives the whisky some depth. A mineral component reminiscent of pebbles resonates and reinforces the earthy character. The intensity lasts a long time without changing significantly.
conclusion: This Kilchoman Batch Strength 2024 delivers exactly what you expect from a robust Islay: pure smoke, a generous helping of drinking pleasure, and a straightforward, honest statement. While it lacks the exciting depth to become an absolute favorite, it’s definitely a solid companion for fans of rustic peat drams on a campfire evening.

nose: A complex aroma unfolds from the very first nosing: a light, ashy peat smoke rises, accompanied by a fresh citrus note reminiscent of lemon and tart grapefruit. But it doesn't stop at fruit and smoke - a hint of leather and herbs combine with hints of cereals and grassy notes that are almost reminiscent of a dense coniferous forest. A flash of sour apple and unripe plum resonate before creamy caramel and fine herbs round off the aroma.
palate: The complexity continues on the palate. The whisky is oily and intense, with a full, almost creamy mouthfeel. Dried fruit dominates the opening before apple and pear come through. Roasted almonds provide a subtle nuttiness, while menthol provides a cool contrast. And then it's there again - the cold smoke, which is reminiscent of an extinguished ember and perfectly rounds off the symphony of flavours.
finish: The finish is nothing less than spectacular. The blend remains present, with a voluminous depth that is reflected in red fruit flavours, sweet lemon and fine smoke. The wood is perfectly integrated, never over-present, but delicate and elegant.
conclusion: Ultramarine is like an exciting film for the senses – full of surprises that delight and leave you wanting more. The balance between smoky spice and fruity sweetness invites relaxed enjoyment without ever becoming dull. A blend that scores with character and charm – definitely a highlight for lovers with an adventurous spirit.

nose: At first sniff, you feel like you're in a market with the scent of apricots, butterscotch and freshly roasted coffee. A light sea breeze blows past. And then there's that hint of honey that caresses the nose. Coconut? Could also be coconut, perhaps an exotic stall at the market. In any case: a fragrance that arouses curiosity.
palate: The first sip is like jumping from one skyscraper roof to the next - exciting and sweet! Caramel sweets and creamy fudge coat the tongue, and then comes this light smoky note, reminiscent of a campfire evening in a city park. Copper leaf and a pinch of salt provide the necessary spice, while figs and sultanas give a hint of the Orient. A firework of flavours!
finish: The finish is long. Dried fruit and a hint of old oak accompany us on the way home, and there is a delicate hint of milk chocolate at the end.
conclusion: This Compass Box Metropolis brings true city life to the glass – lively, multifaceted, and with a smoky spice that makes you eager for the next sip. It’s not the gentlest dram, but that’s exactly what makes it an exciting companion for those who love whisky with character. An urban delight that takes the palate on a little adventure.

nose: The aroma is a conversation starter. At first, it evokes a gas station – a hint of industrial notes that catch you off guard. Beneath this layer lies a fruity core: overripe cherries, coconut, and a touch of exotic mango, nectarine, and peach. Cashew nuts and a sense of decayed wood add earthy undertones, lending complexity to the bouquet.
palate: On the palate, this whisky is powerful and multifaceted. The 54.90% ABV makes its presence known without overwhelming. Spicy oak takes the lead, supported by deep dark red fruits in the background. Then it takes an unexpected turn: an extreme dryness reminiscent of chalk dominates the mouthfeel, while an outstandingly oily texture makes the whisky almost viscous. A hint of soapiness may polarize, but it adds an intriguing layer to the complexity.
finish: The finish surprises with its discrepancy. While the flavors linger on the tongue, the throat remains almost untouched. Subtle notes of honey, hay, and vanilla provide a gentle softness, although the overall impression feels almost ethereal.
conclusion: A whisky that divides opinion and is great fun – with its unusual blend of industrial notes and exotic fruits, the Cameronbridge 1984 clearly stands out from the crowd. Its distinctive dryness and almost ethereal finish aren’t to everyone’s taste, but they make the character all the more intriguing. For anyone looking for a true whisky adventurer, this dram is a genuine hidden gem.

nose: The complexity of this whisky becomes apparent from the very first nosing. A seductive blend of coconut and vanilla meets fruity notes of plums, apricots and strawberries. These are joined by unripe pears and apples, which bring a slight acidity and lend freshness to the bouquet. Marzipan shimmers through in the background and provides a fine sweetness, while a subtle dryness rounds off the complex impression.
palate: The first sip confirms the impression from the nose - the ‘Gordon’ is an intense, yet smooth whisky. An oily texture carries the vinous notes, which are characterised by peaches, blackberries and strawberries. Despite its strength, it remains mild and balanced. The fruity sweetness harmonises wonderfully with the spicy undertones of the casks and gives the whisky a pleasant depth. The combination of sherry and sweet wine casks creates an exciting balance between fruitiness and flavour.
finish: The finish is strong and present without being too dominant. The oak provides structure and length, while the fine berry notes continue to be noticeable. The whisky finishes subtly and delicately without losing any of its intensity.
conclusion: Nc'Nean succeeds with the Gordon in crafting a fruity and spicy whisky that’s a lot of fun and sits wonderfully in the glass thanks to its oily texture. A charming everyday companion for connoisseurs who aren’t afraid of experiments and enjoy exploring new flavor worlds – here, sustainability meets drinking pleasure.

nose: The first impression is unconventional: there is a hint of musty shoebox and sweaty feet - unusual, but not unpleasant. After a few moments, the bouquet opens up: damp moss, a fresh sea breeze and delicate chalk notes are reminiscent of a stormy coast. The vanilla in the background provides a gentle rounding off and combines the rough Islay terrain with a subtle sweetness.
palate: On the tongue, the Bowmore shows its full strength. A strong ash note dominates at first, before an almost sugary sweetness asserts itself - surprising and fascinating at the same time. Fresh vanilla and pear play with the oily mouthfeel and contribute to the pleasant balance. The smoke always remains present, but without masking the other flavours.
finish: Long, voluminous and warming. The oak note typical of many Islay whiskies is held back here, instead the impression of light-coloured fruit and sugar remains. Despite its strength, the finish is comparatively fresh and light.
conclusion: A smoky adventure with plenty of punch and a sweet playfulness – you can practically feel the stormy Islay coast. This isn’t a mainstream whisky, but a characterful dram full of edges and quirks that excites and leaves you wanting more.

nose: Vanilla custard, apple and a pinch of dried apricot join the crumble cake as if they were having a party in a malty orchard. There's also a hint of hay - yes, hay! Not exactly what you'd expect in a whisky, but hey, who needs conventions? This aroma is so inviting that you almost forget to drink the whisky.
palate: The first sip is like a sugary-sweet kiss from the orchard. Apricot and hay dance on the tongue, while the soft oak snuggles up to your taste buds like a good friend. The texture is creamy and oily, as if the whisky had decided to bathe itself in rape honey. It's sweet, but not overpowering - like a compliment from someone who really means what they say.
finish: The finish is medium to long and leaves you with a smile. The oak makes its presence felt once again, accompanied by a light spiciness and a hint of fruitiness. The honey remains until the end.
conclusion: This Glenfarclas is like a sunny afternoon on a Scottish meadow – sweet, charming, and with just enough oak character to keep things interesting. A whisky that’s fun and tells stories without trying too hard. For anyone looking for enjoyment with a wink, this one’s a bullseye.

product_name: Blended Scotch Whisky 40 Years (2024) - Living Souls
nose: The exciting complexity of this whisky is revealed from the very first nosing. Exotic fruits such as papaya, apricot and orange rise to the nose, accompanied by a subtle maritime breeze. Cool smoke is subtly noticeable - not intrusive, but harmoniously embedded. A fine citrus note, perhaps lemon, brings additional freshness. There are also hints of heather and a subtle spiciness. The aroma is not overly intense, but extremely inviting and complex.
palate: On the palate, the whisky unfolds in all its splendour: oily, voluminous and wonderfully balanced. The tropical notes continue - pineapple and mandarin provide a juicy fruitiness, while a subtle spice of white pepper adds excitement. The smoke remains present but restrained - cold and finely interwoven with a nutty note of roasted almonds. A hint of BBQ sauce adds a slightly sweet and savoury nuance that rounds off the overall impression perfectly.
finish: The finish is medium to long, with a great balance of wood, earth and freshness. The oak is present but not dominant, rather it provides a pleasant structure. An unexpected but extremely exciting effect is the minty-fresh smoky note that is noticeable in the aftertaste. There are also fine pine needles and a slight saltiness, which give the whisky an interesting maritime depth.
conclusion: A 40-years whisky that surprises not only with its freshness but also delights with lively fruitiness and a hint of smoke – drinking this is simply a pleasure! Living Souls has managed to combine elegance and character without falling into boring oak clichés. For anyone seeking a multi-layered yet never dull dram, this blend is a real winner.

product_name: Islay Blended Malt Scotch Whisky Kildalton 19 Years (2024) - Living Souls
nose: As soon as you raise the glass, you know: There's a fire here. Wisps of bacon smoke, barbecue charcoal, liquorice and espresso fill the nose. These are joined by pulled pork, honey and a hint of coniferous wood. An intense, yet harmonious flavour.
palate: The palate continues just as powerfully: warm embers, earthy tones, honey-bacon and dark cherries. The combination of sweet smoke and red fruit is simply brilliant. This is joined by salty olives, a strong peaty note and a pleasant spiciness that rounds off the flavour. The 42% alcohol is well integrated and ensures a full-bodied, warm mouthfeel.
finish: The finish is long and voluminous, but still pleasantly soft. Sweet oak, liquorice and a hint of tobacco linger as the smoke slowly evaporates. Not a brutal finish, but one with style - smoky, sweet and with just the right amount of spice.
conclusion: This Kildalton 2024 brings together the smoky Islay power with a surprisingly sweet, almost gentle side – a true pleasure that leaves you wanting more. For fans of character and drinkability, it’s a bullseye, delivering aromas of honey, bacon, and dark fruit that simply make it fun to enjoy.

product_name: Ledaig and Tobermory 18 Years (2024) - Living Souls
nose: The first impression: earthy, cold smoke, as if a burnt-out campfire had been left on a damp meadow. This is joined by spicy heather and old forest honey, which gives the fragrance a pleasant depth. There are subtle flashes of vanilla and a hint of ripe apple in the background. Interestingly, there is even a subtle hint of camembert - an unusual but exciting detail.
palate: The attack is creamy and gentle, almost surprisingly soft. Maritime notes resonate, accompanied by lemongrass and a subtle freshness. The smoke is present, but rather restrained - not the rough peaty club, but a subtle, ashy veil. A slight rubbery note comes through, reminiscent of young Islay whiskies, but it remains in the background. Some tequila-like spice and sea salt round off the experience.
finish: The finish is of medium length and again shows the typical ashy notes of a mature Ledaig. There are also gentle oak tones and a subtle aftertaste of apple and pear.
conclusion: This Ledaig & Tobermory 2024 is like a peaceful walk by the sea – smoky, but never overpowering. Those expecting wild peat monsters will instead discover a charming, restrained character that delights with its gentle spice and surprising details like Camembert, making it a joy to drink. A whisky that impresses with its elegant balance between power and subtlety.

product_name: Blended Scotch Whisky 15 Years (2024) - Living Souls
nose: The very first aroma surprises with a fresh, lively note. Honey and a malty, almost cereal-like sweetness dominate, accompanied by a hint of red fruit and a delicate orange marmalade. Berries and red fruits lend the bouquet a fruity liveliness, while a light chocolate note and a hint of rye provide depth. Complex and inviting.
palate: The whisky is creamy and spicy on the palate. Grapefruit and a delicate dark chocolate harmonise with a subtle date sweetness and a hint of black pepper. Red fruits and brown sugar provide a pleasant sweetness, while turmeric and a dry spiciness round off the flavour. A nice balance between sweet and strong.
finish: The finish is tingling and lively, with a pleasant gingerbread spice and a slightly oaky note. Medium-long and sweet, it nevertheless remains balanced without being overpowering.
conclusion: A whisky that’s a lot of fun, delighting with its spicy depth and sweet-fruity notes – perfect for enjoyable evenings and fans of complex yet approachable blends. The lively character and subtle sherry touch deliver drinking pleasure with plenty of punch. Add a scoop of hazelnut ice cream, and the evening is saved!

product_name: Ruadh Maor (2011/2024) Jack the Ripper - Malts of Scotland
nose: The Ruadh Maor reveals its complexity from the very first sniff. Dark fruits such as ripe plums and a hint of blackberries mingle with an intense smoke that is strongly reminiscent of Islay whiskies. The iodised, almost medicinal notes are also striking - an impression of plaster and camomile pervades the aroma. A green apple brings a subtle freshness, while warm, woody notes of sandalwood emphasise the depth.
palate: The first sip is a powerful prelude: Spicy oak notes meet sweet liquorice and the dark fruits from the nose. The smoke remains present, accompanied by a maritime saltiness that reinforces the Islay feeling. A hint of forest honey provides an unexpected, pleasant sweetness and perfectly balances the spicy intensity.
finish: In the finish, the whisky remains maritime and spicy, but without being too overpowering. The saltiness gives it a refreshing finish, while the smoke lingers for a short time. The finish is of medium length and leaves a balanced memory of wood, smoke and sea breeze.
conclusion: A Highland whisky that really lifts the spirits with its smoky power play and sweet sherry note. Ruadh Maor 2011/2024 Jack the Ripper isn’t a cuddly dram, but a characterful one that delights with maritime freshness and robust smoke – perfect for anyone who likes to try something different while still focusing on enjoyment.

nose: This whisky doesn't mince its words from the very first nosing. What emerges from the glass is a true sherry grenade. A heavy, almost dramatic combination of wet carpet, leather and tobacco meets the autumnal freshness of damp leaves. This is joined by the sweet darkness of burnt caramel. It is a fragrance that tells stories - smoky, earthy and deeply rooted in decades of ageing.
palate: The true magic of this whisky unfolds on the tongue. The first thing you notice is a distinctive dryness reminiscent of a fine sherry. This is accompanied by an amazingly oily texture that takes over the entire mouth. The flavours are intense and clear: fig, rich and dark, is accompanied by earthy leather and bitter cocoa. There is no room for restraint here - this whisky has character and shows it.
finish: The finish is the icing on the cake of this complex drop. It is long, as befits a whisky aged for 20 years, and has a lovely spiciness that finishes astringent and dry. But before it disappears, a slight fruity sweetness flashes through.
conclusion: This Macallan 2003/2023 is a true taste experience – intense, with a powerful sherry explosion that makes every sip exciting. It demands your attention but rewards you with a complex blend of spice, sweetness, and character that’s simply enjoyable. For connoisseurs seeking a whisky with bite and soul, this is a real bullseye!

product_name: Meikle Tòir 5 Years The Chinquapin One (2023)
nose: The first impression is complex. Warm charcoal smoke rises, accompanied by sweet, nutty roasted almonds. A deep spiciness unfolds with cinnamon and ginger, while green apple and malty sweetness add freshness. After the first sip, a soft hint of peach emerges.
palate: The whisky is full-bodied and intense. A smoldering grill fire sets the foundation, over which sweet BBQ notes and spicy cinnamon unfold. Dark caramel and a distinctive teriyaki sauce note enhance the sweet-savory character.
finish: The finish is long and powerfully smoky. Sweet smoke-kissed almonds linger, while cinnamon returns – this time with a touch of chili for an exciting heat.
conclusion: Here, smoky Speyside becomes an experience – young, yet already full of character and with a spiciness that’s genuinely enjoyable. The Chinquapin oak delivers just the right kick to keep the whisky exciting without overwhelming it. Absolutely recommended for fans of powerful yet playful smokiness!

nose: The first sniff reveals a sweetish fragrance accompanied by light sulphur notes. This is accompanied by fruity flavours of raspberry and a subtle hint of caramel. A hint of mould and sourdough rounds off the aroma profile and gives the whisky a certain complexity.
palate: A harmonious interplay of honey and marzipan unfolds on the tongue, supported by a light wine note. There is a subtle astringency and a slight bitterness that lends depth to the flavour. A slightly gamy undertone provides an unusual, unpleasant flavour nuance.
finish: The finish is of medium length and leaves an impression of ripe grapes and a slightly sweet note.
conclusion: The Betz Sauternes Cask Batch 1 is a pleasant whisky with sweet fruitiness and charming marzipan notes – but somehow it lacks the wow factor. Those who enjoy experimental whisky styles and have fun with unusual cask finishes can definitely give this a try, yet genuine depth is hard to find here.

nose: The first impression is a slap in the face. Instead of a harmonious bouquet, you are met with a spirit-heavy attack that is more reminiscent of cheap booze than a carefully matured whisky. Hidden somewhere in between are notes of raspberry, aniseed and - yes, you read that right - Fisherman's Friend cherry. Who came up with that, please? It doesn't smell like a handcrafted whisky, it smells like a failed experiment in chemistry class.
palate: The next disaster reveals itself on the tongue. The whisky tastes sweet, but not in a good way. It is a dull, cereal sweetness accompanied by a faint citrus flavour. But instead of coming across as fresh and lively, the flavour remains flat and uninspired. Where is the depth? Where is the complexity? Instead, you get a flavour that is as exciting as a dry bread roll.
finish: The finish is so short that one is almost grateful - if it wasn't so sharp and woody. The oak dominates here in the worst possible way, accompanied by a sharp afterburn that is more reminiscent of cheap grain than a single cask whisky. There is no long, pleasant finish, just an abrupt end that leaves you perplexed.
conclusion: If you're looking for a whisky that's fun and full of character, you should definitely keep looking rather than choosing the Betz Rye Single Cask 50. Instead of enjoyment, it delivers more of a hangover vibe with a spirit-heavy aroma and a finish that brings no pleasure – better pick another dram!

nose: The nose does indeed begin interestingly: citrus notes, almonds and a slight raspberry sweetness mingle with dark fruits and sultanas. A hint of rum-grape-nut-chocolate gives hope that something special is waiting here. But then comes the disillusionment - the flavours are superficial and quickly evaporate. The promised sherry bomb? Not a chance. Instead, a sharp, almost aggressive note dominates, clouding the overall impression.
palate: The first sip confirms our fears: Boring. The flavour is flat and uninspired. There are hints of dark chocolate, a slight sweetness and even a hint of Mon Chéri chocolates, but everything remains pale and underdeveloped. The PX influences are barely noticeable and the whisky does not manage to be full-bodied. Instead, an oaky dryness dominates, leaving only emptiness instead of complexity.
finish: The finish is short and unpleasantly sharp. Pepper and cocoa try to assert themselves, but here too there is a lack of depth and balance. The spiciness lingers persistently in the mouth and leaves an unpleasant aftertaste that is more reminiscent of cheap spirits than a carefully matured single cask.
conclusion: Unfortunately, the Betz PX Single Cask 52 falls far short of expectations – despite the promising nose, it lacks character and drinking pleasure. It's a shame that four years of maturation and a PX cask yielded nothing more than a flat, somewhat sharp finish. Those who favor German whisky would be better off moving on.

nose: The nose is initially greeted by an interesting blend of pepper and spicy herbs. Underneath, notes of hazelnut and a hint of caramel shimmer, combining with an unexpected sweetness of candy floss and raspberry. The fragrance is complex and multi-layered, raising hopes for an equally multi-layered flavour.
palate: A creamy cream caramel initially unfolds on the tongue, accompanied by soft vanilla and orange tones. But then comes the turnaround: notes of leather and cocoa mix with bitter herbs that dominate the flavour. While the first few seconds still seem harmonious, the whisky quickly becomes flat and one-sided. The bitterness overpowers the finer nuances and makes the flavour less enjoyable.
finish: The finish is creamy and of medium length, with an aftertaste of orange and herbs. Unfortunately, the bitterness remains present here too, which detracts from the otherwise pleasant finish. There is a lack of depth and balance to really round off the whisky.
conclusion: The Betz Master Blend Batch 4 entices with intriguing aromas and a hint of sweetness, but it quickly loses its charm in the glass. Bitterness takes over, making the drinking experience more of a chore than a pleasure – a shame, as it had so much more potential!

nose: Ballechin reveals its smoky soul from the very first sniff. Black BBQ and an extremely bacon-y character are dominant, immediately evoking associations with smoked meat. Underneath, notes of tar, lime and even a hint of burnt rubber resonate. It's like entering an old factory - industrial, raw and yet fascinating.
palate: On the tongue, the Scotch unfolds with a warming, full load of vapour. The flavour is intense and complex: old machine oil, warm rubber tyres and even a hint of linoleum make this whisky a real experience. The smokiness dominates, but it is accompanied by a subtle fruitiness that provides a surprise.
finish: The finish is long and voluminous, with a spicy note that lingers for a long time. The subtle oak is noticeable, but is overlaid by the intense smokiness.
conclusion: Anyone trying the Ballechin 2004/2023 19 Years should have no fear of intense aromas – this whisky is a smoky rollercoaster ride packed with character and plenty of drinking pleasure. For fans of powerful, bacon-like notes, it’s a real highlight that tells stories and leaves you wanting more. Gentle sippers might be better off moving on.

nose: On first nosing, the Glenlivet Nadurra unfolds a fascinating spectrum: floral notes mingle with the warm scent of honey and shortbread, reminiscent of a classic afternoon tea. A slight hint of spicy oak and candy floss joins in, with the latter offering almost bourbon-like echoes. A promising start that prepares the senses for a sweet but not overloaded whisky.
palate: The Nadurra shows its strength from the very first sip. White sugar and coconut notes dominate at first, before a light spiciness and the fruity character of white peach assert themselves. This is accompanied by a creamy cream caramel note and an oily, viscous texture that makes the whisky very present. The white pepper in the finish reinforces the spiciness and provides a warming finish.
finish: The finish of the Glenlivet Nadurra is surprisingly short to medium in length. Fresh oak notes dominate, accompanied by a hint of honey and minimal light fruity flavours. Although pleasant, it lacks the depth that many connoisseurs might expect from such a strong whisky.
conclusion: The Glenlivet Nadurra 2015 is a powerful Speyside whisky that really lifts the spirits with its sweet oiliness and intense aroma. Despite its strength, it drinks surprisingly easily and offers drinking pleasure without major hurdles. However, it lacks that certain something that would make it unforgettable – a good dram that just misses a bit of depth to truly crown it.

nose: From the very first nosing, it becomes clear that this whisky has a story to tell. Caramel, as intense as melted sugar on the hob, meets velvety vanilla and sweet honey. A subtle note of soft oak resonates, accompanied by a hint of stone fruit that lends a fruity depth. Surprising and slightly unorthodox is a note reminiscent of thinner or touch-up pencil - an exciting contrast.
palate: A mixture of fresh wood and damp sawdust unfolds on the palate, providing a rustic touch. The sweetness comes to the fore in the form of cream caramels, giving the whisky a soft and almost creamy texture. Despite the complexity, the flavour is not overloaded, but balanced, with a slightly sweet base note reminiscent of patisserie.
finish: In the finish, the North British is surprisingly restrained. A slight warmth spreads, but disappears relatively quickly.
conclusion: This North British 31-years offers an exciting rollercoaster of sensations: sweet, caramel-rich, yet surprisingly light. For connoisseurs seeking whisky with character and a touch of idiosyncrasy, this is a real find – even if the lightness in the finish feels a bit prickly.

nose: The first nosing reveals a fascinating medley of red fruits – sour cherries, dates, and figs lead the way. This is complemented by an elegant woody note reminiscent of driftwood, coupled with a slightly burnt character leaning towards cigar tobacco. This balance of sweet and earthy tones makes the bouquet remarkably layered.
palate: The first sip is an experience: full-bodied and powerful, a pleasant sweetness greets the palate. The creamy, soft texture reveals notes of leather, cocoa powder, and dried plums. Subtle nutty tones play in the background, while oak takes on a leading role without dominating the profile. Each drop tells the story of a sherry cask transformed by time and care.
finish: The finish is long and multi-layered: a sweet-spicy warmth spreads, accompanied by dry oak and nutty flavors, particularly walnuts. What remains is a harmonious, balanced finish that leaves the impression of a perfectly matured malt.
conclusion: A whisky that immediately wins your heart with its creamy sweetness and spicy sherry notes – simply a pleasure that relaxes and delights. The 20 years of maturation give it character and depth without feeling overdone. Perfect for connoisseurs looking for a warm, rounded dram full of personality.

product_name: Aberfeldy 18 Years (2021) French Red Wine Casks
nose: Even the nosing of this whisky is an experience. Notes of dates and figs rise to the nose, carried by a gentle hint of light-coloured grapes and clove. An interesting ‘sandy’ undertone is reminiscent of a walk in an autumn landscape - earthy, slightly rough and inviting at the same time. After the first sip, increasingly fruity flavours unfold, intensifying the impression.
palate: On the tongue, the Aberfeldy 18 years is initially creamy - almost like a velvety wine. But then the Highlands make their unmistakable mark: oak and a hint of coloured pepper provide a spicy base, accompanied by subtle notes of red fruit. The slightly vinous note from the maturation in French red wine casks adds an elegant dimension without being too dominant.
finish: And then comes the finish - wow! The interplay of flavours reaches its climax here. A clear strawberry note leads into raspberry ice cream before everything dissolves into a creamy, spicy warmth. The strong spiciness leaves a lasting impression that lingers on the palate for minutes afterwards.
conclusion: Aberfeldy 18 years impressively demonstrates how a whisky can benefit from spicy sherry notes and fruity complexity. Particularly fascinating: with time in the glass, it gains in fruitiness, which makes every new tasting an experience. Lovers of intense and complex whiskies will be more than satisfied. This whisky is not a simple sipper - it invites you to engage with it and experience the development of its flavours. The Aberfeldy 2021, 18 years French Red Wine Casks harmonises perfectly with duck breast in a red wine reduction, as the fruity notes of the whisky elegantly pick up on the flavours of wine and meat. For dessert, raspberry ice cream with dark chocolate complements the berry and creamy finish.

nose: From the very first sniff, Deanston catapults your nose into a tropical fruit basket. Imagine opening a fresh tin of peaches and apricots, sprinkling a few pieces of pineapple on top and topping it all off with honey sweetness and a hint of whipped cream. A gentle touch of creamy caramel and a small herbal greeting à la mate tea round off the experience. Sounds fancy? It is - but without being lifted off.
palate: This is where it gets really exciting! The Deanston packs quite a punch at 55.7 % vol. but without overwhelming the palate. The oily texture is like silk on the tongue, while lemon balm and heather add fresh accents. The tropical fruits from the nose are also present here - apricot, pineapple and the like make an appearance. At the same time, a good portion of oak provides spice and depth. It is a balance of power and elegance that is simply fun.
finish: The finish is like the final act of a good film: warm, dry and a little pensive. The oak comes to the fore and brings spicy, almost tart notes, while the fruitiness slowly fades away. A hint of tea lingers - a subtle finish that immediately invites you to take the next sip.
conclusion: This Deanston 11 Years from Best Dram is a true fruit bomb that delights with its rum finish and never becomes boring. The tropical aromas and oily texture turn every sip into a little holiday flirt – perfect for anyone who wants to have fun with whisky without spending a lot of money.

nose: The nose is a real adventure! Strawberries? Check. Red fruits and berries? There too. A vinous bouquet gently reminiscent of a summer evening in Tuscany. But then comes the surprise: a hint of yoghurt creeps in, paired with a delicate hint of grey bread. Yes, grey bread - and no, that's not a typo. Sounds crazy, but smells brilliant.
palate: With the first sip, you immediately know why this whisky is fun: strawberry yoghurt, sweet and fruity, dominates the start. But before it becomes too sweet, peppery spice and a pleasant dryness bring balance. Dazu gesellen sich Karamell und Honig – wie ein Werther’s Original, aber mit Stil. Es ist, als würde man sich in einer französischen Patisserie befinden, während ein Highland-Cow einem freundlich zunickt.
finish: The finish is of medium length and is characterised by spicy oak with a subtle winey note, reminiscent of a good glass of red wine. Perfect for anyone who likes to enjoy their whiskies with a touch of elegance.
conclusion: Who would have thought a whisky could be so playful and yet elegant? The Edradour 2012/2024 Frühburgunder surprises with its fruity lightness and charming strawberry yogurt twist – a true delight for the palate that livens up any tasting. Perfect if you're in the mood for a bit of unconventional drinking fun!

nose: As soon as you open the bottle, you are greeted by an intense aroma. Fruity-sweet with a slightly artificial note reminiscent of cherry lollipops, combined with a hint of sultanas and nutty flavours. This is joined by a warm spiciness of slightly burnt wood, which immediately arouses the nose's curiosity.
palate: The first sip confirms that this whisky is no ordinary representative. The flavour is fruity and berry, with a slightly bitter undertone of tobacco and leather. Walnut notes provide a pleasant depth that is not too heavy, but not too light either. The 50.10 % vol. is noticeable but well integrated - a whisky that shows character without setting the throat on fire.
finish: In the finish, the Sild Flören shows its spicy, chocolaty side. The fruity notes recede into the background, replaced by fresh tobacco leaves, a pleasant bitterness and a hint of spice.
conclusion: A whisky that really lifts the spirits with its powerful 50.1% ABV and maritime maturation on Sylt. Fruity, nutty, and with a surprisingly spicy finish, the Sild Flören brings fun to the glass – perfect for those who love character without being overwhelmed.

nose: The Delos impresses with a wonderfully fresh bouquet from the very first sniff. Intense notes of ripe pear, honey and vanilla dominate, accompanied by delicate oak and a slightly resinous undertone. A hint of lemongrass and floral accents, combined with maltiness à la butter biscuits, creates an almost tropical atmosphere: dragon fruit, apricot and pineapple combine to create a waxy, elegant flavour that invites you to savour it.
palate: The sophistication continues seamlessly on the palate. The Compass Box Delos is ultra-creamy, soft and round, with an impressive complexity. Old oak provides an elegant structure, while the fruity pear and citrus notes retain freshness. Floral nuances and subtle herbal spice add depth, while the cereal notes pay homage to bourbon maturation.
finish: The medium-length finish leaves a pleasant echo of intense oak. Green tea rounds off the experience and adds a slightly bitter note that emphasises the full-bodied character of Delos.
conclusion: With the Compass Box Delos, you experience a blend that’s simply a joy – creamy, fruity, and full of character, inviting both enjoyment and admiration. For anyone looking for a whisky with charm and a touch of history, it’s a genuine highlight not to be missed.

product_name: St. Kilian Single Cask – Irish Style – Unpeated
nose: A fresh opening with intense fruity notes. Citrus aromas like apple peel and lemon zest blend with a lightly perfumed floral hint. The alcohol stays discreetly in the background.
palate: Ultra smooth yet remarkably fresh. A touch of fruity funk runs through, with pineapple blossoms and green apples standing out. The balance between sweetness and zesty freshness is pleasantly maintained.
finish: Medium-long with fruity accents of apple and lemon zest, accompanied by gentle oak. The aftertaste is clean, leaving a harmonious overall impression.
conclusion: This St. Kilian Irish Style is a genuine delight, whose lightness and fruity freshness instantly lift the mood. Despite its 57.4% ABV, it feels pleasantly smooth and invites relaxed sipping – almost an elegant surprise coup from Germany.

nose: The first impression is characterized by tangy red fruits and sweet honey. Light hay notes, vanilla, and mild oak follow. A hint of baking powder, cinnamon, and candle wax rounds off the aromatic profile, adding a subtle spiciness.
palate: Smooth and creamy, the Macallan 12 Double Cask unfolds on the palate. Honey sweetness dominates at first, followed by grassy tones, plum, and a pinch of white pepper. Cinnamon adds a warming spice that complements the overall impression harmoniously.
finish: The finish is rather short, with light oak spice and gentle dryness. A subtle wood note appears but fades quickly.
conclusion: If you're looking for a gentle, straightforward whisky that scores with honeyed sweetness and warm sherry notes, the Macallan 12 Year Double Cask will bring you joy. For me, it’s a pleasant companion that doesn’t offer any big surprises but precisely because of that fits nicely in the glass – a solid everyday whisky with character that doesn’t annoy.

product_name: Ardmore 15 Years (2007/2023) Mouton bouillie Cask 66.239 - The Scotch Malt Whisky Society
nose: From the very first breath, this whisky transports the senses to a freshly heated sauna, where the warm wood fills the room. A hint of ripe pears and freshly cut hay mingle with an invigorating note of green tea. It's like walking across a freshly mown meadow in summer, while the clear mountain air refreshes the spirit.
palate: The whisky is oily and smooth on the tongue, with a sweet honey note reminiscent of dark forest honey. But then it becomes deeper, almost shadier - the sweet notes give way to a hint of pine cones and a slightly mineral freshness.
finish: The finish is not intrusive, but medium-long and elegant. A slight tingle is reminiscent of the spicy intensity of the Highlands, while the sweet pear note lingers discreetly.
conclusion: A whisky that immediately wins your heart with its warm wooden sauna character and sweet honey notes. The combination of freshness and depth makes it a charming companion that leaves you wanting more – perfect for relaxed evenings with good food.

nose: The first impression of the Miyagikyo is marked by a pleasant sweetness, with notes of banana and delicate vanilla. The smoky character is subtle but still noticeable, adding depth to the aroma. There's also a slightly earthy note reminiscent of potatoes—a mix of earth and peel, giving the nose a wild edge. Alongside this, malty undertones form a solid base for the aroma.
palate: On the palate, the Miyagikyo is exceptionally creamy and smooth. The flavors unfold with a juicy, lightly fruity profile, with apple and a fine fruit sweetness standing out. There's a pleasant mix of sweet and lightly bitter tones that combine for a fascinating flavor experience. The smoky influence adds extra body to the whisky, while grain and a touch of brown sugar come through. The finish brings a slight bitterness that isn't overpowering and nicely rounds off the whisky's complexity.
finish: The finish of the Miyagikyo is medium-length, with a mild but still present smoky character. It follows with a robust oak spice that gives the whisky structure, along with a hint of white pepper, tickling the palate and keeping the finish lively.
conclusion: A whisky that simply delights with its creamy sweetness and subtle smoke – perfect for relaxed evenings without any fuss. The slight earthiness gives the Miyagikyo a charming character that doesn’t come across as too polished but remains lively. For fans of a gentle yet characterful single malt, this dram is definitely worth a sip.

nose: From the very first whiff, a distinctive cold, almost pavement-like smoke rises to the nose, paving the way for a dominant nuttiness. The flavours of roasted hazelnuts and almonds merge with a fruity magic that is mainly carried by ripe plums. This composition is accompanied by a hint of old leather, nutmeg and the spicy scent of old wood - as if you were sitting in a decades-old study surrounded by books and history.
palate: The first sip unfolds oily and full-bodied in the mouth, a sensual texture that immediately reaches every corner of the palate. The cold iodine smoke meets a fruity sweetness that is intense and multi-layered. The notes of plums and dates combine with leather and tobacco to offer a deep, almost mystical flavour experience. A slight saltiness emphasises the whole and gives the whisky a maritime note reminiscent of the rugged coast of Scotland - and all this from the heart of Germany. With a few drops of water, the profile becomes even more complex: Morello cherries come to the fore, while the smoke gently recedes and gives way to a wild mix of berries. It's like discovering a new whisky in an old guise.
finish: The finish is of medium length, with the smoke still present and accompanied by fruity notes of plum and cherry. It is a finish that does not end abruptly, but fades gently, like a slow sunset over the hills - warm, calming and full of depth.
conclusion: The Westfalian TW74 captivates with its smoky depth and fruity accents – a genuine pleasure that leaves you wanting more. This combination of plum jam and nutty warmth delivers pure drinking enjoyment without ever becoming dull. For anyone seeking a whisky with character and heart, this dram is a bullseye.

product_name: St. Kilian Bud Spencer - The Legend - Rauchig - Batch 03
nose: A cold, smoky aroma immediately takes center stage, reminiscent of smoldering campfire embers. Beneath the smoke lies a layer of green apple and a fleeting hint of exotic fruits. Over time, vanilla and caramel emerge, providing a balancing sweetness.
palate: On the palate, the whisky delivers a bold spiciness with a kick of white pepper. Its texture is creamy yet dry, complemented by a herbal freshness reminiscent of menthol. The smoke dominates, leaving little room for fruity notes, though a faint hint of banana makes an appearance. The whisky’s youthful edge is noticeable but not overpowering.
finish: The finish is robustly spicy with prominent oak notes. The smoke lingers persistently, accompanied by a subtle exotic fruit residue and a touch of caramel. An intense and smoky conclusion.
conclusion: This smoky St. Kilian Bud Spencer impresses with its powerful presence and straightforward drinking pleasure – perfect for anyone who likes their whisky bold and direct. The Amarone note stays in the background, which only makes the dram feel more honest and approachable. A whisky that delights with charm, character, and rough edges, doing justice to Bud Spencer’s rugged persona.

product_name: St. Kilian Sie nannten ihn Spencer – Unpeated
nose: From the first sniff, a bouquet of intense red fruits reminiscent of juicy plum jam unfolds. Enticing notes of dark chocolate and a hint of macadamia nuts follow. A subtle wine aroma adds depth, while a touch of mint provides a refreshing twist. This complex aromatic profile is a true fruit explosion with harmonious elegance.
palate: On the palate, the whisky reveals a bold, oily, and sweet character. Dominant flavors of dark grapes and red wine merge with a rich herbal spiciness. Dried fruits and bitter chocolate add further depth and balance. The texture remains velvety and pleasantly smooth.
finish: The medium-long finish is warming and slightly sticky, accompanied by intense oak spice and a hint of lovage. Red fruits linger until the end, giving the whisky a charming freshness.
conclusion: This St. Kilian impresses with a powerful intensity and a hearty character that turns every sip into an experience – just like Bud Spencer himself. Despite its youthful maturity, the choice of cask provides an exciting depth and a fruity fireworks display that leaves you wanting more. A whisky that simply brings joy with its personality and warmth.

nose: From the first nosing, the whisky's 18 years in oak casks are unmistakable. A warm spiciness rises, underscored by sweet almonds and dried orange peel. Light leather and subtle banana chips add an elegant depth. In the background, a touch of honey rounds out the aromatic spectrum.
palate: On the palate, the anCnoc 18 Years is creamy yet dry. European oak asserts itself with prominent tannins, giving the whisky structure and fullness. Fruity notes of apricot and juicy raisins meet delicate milk chocolate. Dates provide a gentle sweetness, harmoniously blending with the whisky’s woody foundation.
finish: The finish is medium-length and warming, characterized by toasted oak and a pleasant spiciness from white pepper. A hint of raisins lingers on the tongue, accompanied by a touch of baking cocoa. A satisfying finale that invites the next sip.
conclusion: The anCnoc 18 Years is a true delight for anyone seeking a whisky that is both spicy and gentle on the finish. The fine balance of sweet fruits and spicy oak makes it the perfect companion for relaxed evenings – simply a delicious easy sipper with character and heart.

nose: The first impression? A sultana explosion straight to the face - like opening a bag of Christmas cheer! Caramel and soft oak emphasise this sweet and spicy base, while earthier notes of tobacco and brown sugar tickle the nose. If you sniff closely, you will even detect a hint of peanuts, which gives the fragrance a nutty depth.
palate: The whisky is viscous, almost syrupy on the tongue - a mouthfeel that triggers feelings of pure luxury. Malty notes dominate at the beginning before a symphony of sultanas, dates and candied apple unfolds. A touch of caramel provides a pleasant sweetness, while the nutty peanut flavour provides a contrast. Anyone who enjoys rich, full-bodied whiskies could go into raptures here.
finish: Medium length with roasted almonds and cloves. Candied fruit adds a spicy twist, and there is a sudden flash of tropical freshness from mango and papaya.
conclusion: This Ben Nevis really packs a punch – the sherry explosion and the full cask strength make it a genuine experience for connoisseurs who enjoy intensity and character. A whisky that’s a lot of fun and truly comes alive when paired with dark chocolate or fruitcake. Highly recommended for anyone looking for a robust, lively Highland dram!

product_name: Teaninich 11 Years Malaga Cask Finish - James Eadie
nose: A potpourri of fruit flavours unfolds from the very first nosing: mango, grapefruit and peach characterise the opening and give the whisky a pleasant tropical freshness. These notes are accompanied by a hint of caramel and a nutty flavour of almond and marzipan. The light hints of milk chocolate are particularly charming, adding a subtle sweetness. An inviting start that is uncomplicated and harmonious.
palate: On the palate, the Teaninich develops a pleasant freshness, carried by green grapes and red fruits, which give it a lively, fruity flavour. The whisky is wonderfully full-bodied and has a warming presence. The base is reminiscent of fresh cereals - perhaps even a slightly sweet Bircher muesli - with a slightly straw-like and toasted undertone. This combination gives the whisky structure and depth without making it too heavy.
finish: The finish is long and balanced, with an unexpected hint of cheese curd that gives the whisky a creamy and slightly sweet component. Light tannins add a touch of dryness, while wet coffee powder and herbal tea add a spicy finish. A successful finish that is not too intrusive and makes you want to take the next sip.
conclusion: A whisky that’s simply fun and spreads good cheer with its fruity lightness. The Malaga finish adds a charming twist that neither overwhelms nor bores – perfect for relaxed evenings or as a sweet companion to desserts. Not the most complex Highland, but precisely for that reason so likeable and approachable.

product_name: The English Whisky Infinity Cask - Distillery Exclusive (2024) Batch 3
nose: Fresh and crisp, reminiscent of a spring meadow. Subtle hints of forest honey and beeswax add a sweet, natural depth without overpowering the profile.
palate: The initial impression is creamy and mild, with a pleasant sweetness of honey and sugary undertones. Fruity notes of apricot and peach gently come to the fore. A slight warmth develops over time, harmonizing the overall flavor.
finish: The finish is short but delightful. Sweet and fruity notes linger briefly, followed by a light dryness with a touch of white pepper, creating an elegant conclusion.
conclusion: This Infinity Cask is like a lively stroll full of surprises – sweet, fruity, and with a charming hint of roughness that gives it character. It’s simply fun to sip your way through the flavors without it ever feeling too complicated. For anyone looking for a whisky that stays vibrant yet remains pleasantly approachable, this comes highly recommended.

nose: The Dailuaine reveals its complexity from the very first inhalation. A malty base is accompanied by sultanas soaked in rum and a hint of cream and custard. The fragrance is dense and rich, almost heavy. Another inhale and the flavours of buttery red apple crumble, pear and peach syrup emerge. The olfactory experience is rounded off with notes of honey, pear and butter biscuits. A hint of cream caramel and sultanas gives the whisky a pleasant sweetness, which is complemented by hints of apple sauce, vanilla and coconut shell.
palate: On the palate, the Single Malt unfolds with an oily, rich texture. The interplay of apple-pear compote and vanilla custard forms the prelude, followed by a subtle herbal note and a hint of wine gum. The lemon zest adds a refreshing citrus note that harmonises perfectly with the buttery apple crumble. White grape, rum and stewed pear contribute to the fruity depth, while hints of varnished wood and ginger add a spicy complexity. This bottling offers a rare balance between fruit, sweetness and spice that we really like.
finish: The finish of the Speysider is long-lasting and warming. A gentle spiciness, dominated by pepper and ginger, leaves a pleasant aftertaste. Mixed fruits marinated in rum, apple crumble and a subtle woody note round off the experience.
conclusion: A genuine pleasure that’s fun and full of character – the Dailuaine 2012 from Malts of Scotland impresses with its fruity sweetness and buttery apple notes. For me, it’s a bottling you’re happy to pour yourself another glass of, even if it doesn’t quite reach the absolute highlights.

nose: From the very first nosing, it is noticeable that Elsburn The Journey 2020 has unusual flavours. The fragrance brings with it a gentle caramel note that arouses curiosity, accompanied by a slightly musty base that mixes with traces of sulphur and walnut. Added to this is the warm scent of boiled chestnuts, which gives the whisky an autumnal, nutty depth.
palate: On the palate, Elsburn unfolds with a soft, marzipan-like sweetness reminiscent of roasted almonds. A light fruitiness and the warm flavour of forest honey provide a harmonious sweetness, which is quickly replaced by a pinch of sultanas and a subtle roasted aroma. A surprising note is the slightly oily mouthfeel, which gives the whisky more body and at the same time makes it appear rounder.
finish: In the finish, Elsburn The Journey 2020 shows a stronger side: black pepper and a hint of burnt wood bring the experience to a spicy and somewhat rustic end. However, the finish is rather short and tingling - a quick finish that does not leave a long aftertaste, but rounds off the spicy experience nicely.
conclusion: Elsburn The Journey 2020 is a charming German single malt that offers plenty of enjoyment with its nutty sweetness and spicy edges. The youthful, slightly raw notes aren’t entirely polished yet, but that very quality gives it a distinct character that piques curiosity — ideal for anyone looking for an unconventional tasting experience.

nose: This whisky reveals its smoky soul from the very first contact. A dense peaty flavour is combined with notes of tobacco and a fascinating iodine component reminiscent of a maritime adventure. At the same time, a sweet vanilla note resonates, giving the flavour profile a gentle balance.
palate: In the mouth, the Ardmore 2008 unfolds with an oily, almost creamy texture. The campfire flavour is present, but not overwhelming, rather embedded in fruity-salty nuances. The fruitiness is reminiscent of juicy pears and a hint of pineapple, which blend surprisingly harmoniously with the smoky character
finish: The finish is of medium length and leaves a pleasant smoky note, accompanied by salty and maritime tones. It feels like a cool sea breeze after a night by the campfire - a harmonious end.
conclusion: This Ardmore 2008 is a true pleasure seeker: smoke, fruit, and maritime notes flirt with each other here in an effortless way. For anyone craving a robust Highland whisky with character and a touch of campfire romance, this is a real winner – it makes the whisky heart smile!

nose: The nosing starts pleasantly, if unsurprisingly. Vanilla is the first note that comes to the nose - sweet, almost creamy. The sherry cask is also noticeable, but rather discreetly, without the depth and complexity that we know from whiskies aged more heavily in sherry. A hint of spicy oak provides a slightly bitter contrast, but this quickly disappears again. Another interesting nuance is provided by the wet leather, which lends the bouquet a certain earthiness.
palate: The flavour of the Glenfiddich Reserve Cask remains true to its line. The first impression is buttery smooth, which gives it a pleasant drinkability. This is the kind of whisky that can be enjoyed without much thought, perhaps at a social gathering or as an accompaniment to a conversation. The oak comes more to the fore and gives the whisky a little more substance, but here too everything remains within reason. There are no overpowering or polarising flavours - making it ideal for those looking for an uncomplicated pleasure.
finish: The finish, like the rest of the whisky, is sweet and fruity and rather short. The oak gently accompanies the finish without remaining present for a particularly long time. Anyone expecting a long, intense finish will probably be disappointed here, but for a whisky that is clearly designed as a mass product, it remains pleasant and pleasing.
conclusion: A whisky that scores effortlessly with its creamy vanilla and gentle oak notes – perfect for relaxed evenings when you’re not looking for any surprises. It’s enjoyable without demanding too much and has exactly the kind of character you can just sip down easily. For those seeking adventure, however, it’s a bit too tame.

nose: The very first nose contact leaves no doubt: fruit is the trump card here! Walnut meets a pleasant sweetness of pears and apples, accompanied by creamy vanilla. The flavours are like a freshly picked fruit basket - but nothing is overpowering. The bouquet remains subtle and light, a gentle autumn breeze that promises but does not reveal too much.
palate: The first sip takes the whisky in a completely new direction. ‘Wow, that's so sweet’, you might think - and you wouldn't be wrong. Ripe peaches and other stone fruit flavours dominate at first, before an unexpected spiciness enters the stage. This is joined by a hint of cherry and sour apple rings, reminiscent of carefree childhood days. But before it gets too playful, the oak brings everything into balance: powerful, spicy, almost tannic.
finish: The finish is medium-long, not too short, but not a marathon either. It is pleasantly warming and lingers in the memory with a spicy, rich note. A fine, chalky hint creeps in, reminiscent of cool stone walls or freshly polished chalk.
conclusion: This Glenturret 29 Years is a tempting adventure for the senses, delighting with its sweet fruitiness and spicy oak – perfect for cozy evenings with cheese and good company. While it lacks that final spark to truly shine, it’s precisely this quality that makes it both charming and approachable.

nose: The aroma of this whisky is quite restrained at first, almost closed. Some citrus tickles the nose and brings freshness to the slightly dull, restrained structure. Delicate vanilla and a hint of hay unfold underneath, accompanied by a hint of metallic and herbal notes. Overall, the Glenfiddich Select Cask is balanced and pleasant, but remains rather restrained and quiet in its flavour - a fragrance that promises a lot, but does not yet fully reveal itself.
palate: On the palate, the Glenfiddich Select Cask is as smooth as butter. The velvety texture dominates the flavour here, and the whisky actually glides down the tongue without any sharpness or distracting edges. The vanilla flavour blends subtly with light oak and there is a hint of malt. But while the mouthfeel is pleasant, there are no real highlights. The flavour, like the aroma, remains rather pale and restrained, with little depth and hardly any surprising moments.
finish: The finish of the Glenfiddich Select Cask is short and inconspicuous. A brief hint of oak spice flickers up, but before you know it, the last notes evaporate. What remains is an overall impression that is not very lasting, which emphasises the gentle character of the whisky, but unfortunately leaves hardly any reverberation.
conclusion: The Glenfiddich Select Cask is a gentle, unpretentious whisky that, with its soft texture, especially appeals to beginners. However, for those seeking excitement and character, it lacks that certain something – it remains more of a subdued background tune than a true star of enjoyment.

nose: Tannins reminiscent of freshly polished wood meet a pungent citrus note - the lemon here seems more like the cheeky cousin who turns up uninvited to the party. This is joined by a sharp, almost chemical undertone that is slightly reminiscent of spirit. No trace of Caribbean warmth, but rather ‘Wuppertal in November’.
palate: This is where it gets exciting, because the flavours are ... let's say ... idiosyncratic. There are vaguely sweetish notes reminiscent of overcooked sugar cane molasses before the 51% alcohol hits with full force. Light bitter notes dance on the tongue, followed by a woody spiciness and bitterness reminiscent of unripe fruit.
finish: Oh, the finish is indeed impressive - but less in a positive sense. The spiciness really burns itself into the throat and leaves an aftershock that feels like it lasts forever. A rum finish? More like a fire finish. ‘Burns like hell’ sums it up perfectly.
conclusion: If you’re into wild experiments with plenty of punch, this delivers a genuine adventure – unfortunately one that feels more like a bumpy action movie than Caribbean lightness. Betz Jamaica Rum Cask Batch 1 is a dramatic ride that could be fun if it didn’t burn so unpleasantly.

product_name: Allt-a-Bhainne 17 Years (2005) Infrequent Flyers - Alistair Walker Whisky Company
nose: The very first nosing gives an idea that this whisky knows no restraint. Sweet honey notes meet a hint of red wine - a little like a glass of dessert wine on a winter evening. This is joined by the freshness of oranges and a hint of crème brûlée, which rounds off the whole flavour with a light vanilla note. In the background: subtle oak that pleasantly grounds the fragrance.
palate: With the first sip it becomes clear: this whisky has a personality! The attack is surprisingly sweet, almost candy-like, before the red wine influence comes through - fruity and a little tannic. But then comes the twist: a slight smokiness is noticeable, surprising for a Speyside whisky. Fruity notes, especially orange, remain present, but an astringent, slightly dry texture ensures that the whisky is not one-dimensional. The cask strength makes for a powerful experience that is never overpowering.
finish: In the finish, the Allt-a-Bhainne 2005 shows a different side. The sweetness still lingers, but is accompanied by a dry spiciness and a pleasant warmth that slowly recedes. The whisky finishes in style without lingering too long.
conclusion: The Allt-a-Bhainne 2005 Infrequent Flyers is a true character among Speyside whiskies – powerful, sweet, and with a smoky surprise that’s a lot of fun. The cask strength perfectly highlights the fruity and spicy notes, turning every sip into an enjoyable adventure. This is a whisky for connoisseurs who love discovering something new and aren’t looking for the classic flavor profile.

nose: A fruity whirlwind hits you from the very first sniff. Red, ripe apples push their way to the front as if they were about to climb out. They are accompanied by juicy red grapes and a hint of redcurrants - a fruit basket par excellence! But that's not all: a pleasant maltiness and a hint of effervescence ensure that the nose not only sniffs, but dances. Bright fruits and a hint of citrus round off the experience.
palate: One sip and you're at the Christmas market - but not at just any stall, but at the crêpes! The sweetness of cinnamon and sugar harmonises with a creamy apple sauce aroma, as if the best autumn flavours had been captured in a bottle. The whisky feels oily and full-bodied, nestling comfortably on the palate, wonderfully carried by the 46% ABV.
finish: After the last sip, the Royal Brackla remains present for a long time. A slight ginger kick provides a welcome spiciness, which is complemented by subtle oak notes and a hint of dryness. A fine nuttiness floats along.
conclusion: This Royal Brackla 10 Year from James Eadie is like a warm hug on a cool autumn evening – sweet, spicy, and full of character. The fruity aromas and inviting spice make it the perfect companion for social gatherings or a cozy get-together. Absolutely enjoyable without being overly complicated.

nose: The flavour is reminiscent of bacon glazed in honey - sweet, salty, a hint of smoke. Lemon zest and bourbon vanilla give it a fresh, creamy note that harmonises perfectly with the icing sugar sweetness. But then come the maritime notes: Rust, seaweed, and a hint of sea salt. It's like walking over the cliffs on a rough Islay day.
palate: At the first sip, an intense rush of iodine and cold smoke is noticeable, accompanied by a strong salty impact that emphasises the maritime tones.
